clean_story:
Speed versus stealth. Evolution’s endless chess.

The term arms race borrows from human wars—each side upgrading weapons to outmatch the other. But nature invented it first.

A cheetah’s 70 mph sprint isn’t just fast—it’s a reply to a gazelle’s zigzag dodges, honed over millennia. The gazelle’s stotting (high, stiff-legged jumps) isn’t panic; it signals "I’m fit—don’t waste energy chasing me." Like two swordsmen reading each other’s moves, but with muscles and bones as blades.

Bats and moths play a sonic game: bats scream ultrasound to echolocate prey; some moths evolved ears to detect those screams—and even click back to jam the bat’s radar. Others grew furry scales to absorb sound, turning invisible. One species, Bertholdia trigona, fights fire with fire: it clicks at bats mid-hunt, mimicking their own calls to confuse them.

Even plants join in. Tobacco plants, when attacked by caterpillars, release chemicals that attract wasps—which then lay eggs inside the caterpillars. The caterpillars? Some now regurgitate plant toxins to trick wasps into leaving.

No winner, only escalation. A lion’s night vision counters a zebra’s stripe-camouflage; the zebra’s kick can crush a lion’s jaw. Every advantage is temporary.

Three related subjects are co-evolution’s tricks, deception in nature, and human tech copying biology.
